synopsis: David Swensen draws on his experience managing Yale's endowment to lay out a framework for institutional portfolio management centered on broad diversification and a heavy allocation to alternative asset classes like private equity. The book covers investment philosophy, asset allocation strategy, manager selection, and the governance structures that allow endowments to pursue unconventional approaches unavailable to most individual investors.

Michael Dell recounts his journey from founding a PC business in his University of Texas dorm room to leading one of the world's largest technology companies, tracing three defining corporate battles: the company's launch, a fight to preserve it against activist investor Carl Icahn, and its transformation. The memoir examines how Dell matured from entrepreneur to executive while navigating competitors, mentors, and high-stakes takeover conflicts.

sources.title: Play Nice But Win | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Set in a future America where machines have automated most labor, the novel follows Dr. Paul Proteus, an engineer-manager who grows disillusioned with a society that has stratified into a technical elite and a displaced populace stripped of meaningful work. Proteus abandons his privileged position and joins an underground rebellion against the system, while a visiting foreign dignitary witnesses the contradictions of this mechanized utopia.

Former federal prosecutor Brendan Ballou examines how private equity firms acquire businesses across industries—retailers, nursing homes, prison services, and mobile-home parks—using borrowed money and legal structures that insulate them from liability, often leaving those companies bankrupt or hollowed out. The book argues that government policy actively enables these practices and proposes reforms to hold firms accountable.

Using a fictionalized dialogue between two employees, Subir Chowdhury explains the Six Sigma quality management methodology—a data-driven system that combines designated problem-solving teams with rigorous process analysis to reduce defects and improve organizational performance. The book offers a short, accessible introduction to Six Sigma concepts originally advanced at Motorola and popularized by General Electric under Jack Welch.

Nick Lane argues that mitochondria—organelles that evolved from ancient bacterial endosymbionts—are central to understanding the origin of complex life, the evolution of sexual reproduction, biological aging, and certain inherited diseases. The book traces how mitochondrial DNA, inherited maternally and prone to rapid mutation, connects major evolutionary transitions to the processes of senescence and cell death.

sources.title: Power, Sex, Suicide | Oxford University Press

synopsis: Patty McCord, former Chief Talent Officer at Netflix, describes the unconventional people-management philosophy she helped build there, arguing that traditional HR practices—annual reviews, retention programs, and employee engagement initiatives—are largely counterproductive. She advocates instead for radical honesty, building teams of high performers suited to the company's current needs, and treating employees as responsible adults.

Mark Rippetoe presents a systematic approach to strength training programming that distinguishes between the different physiological responses of novice, intermediate, and advanced lifters, explaining why a single periodization model cannot serve all athletes equally. The book covers training variables, the underlying exercise science, and practical programming structures for each level built around the major barbell lifts.

sources.title: Practical Programming for Strength Training | Amazon

synopsis: Robert Cialdini argues that effective persuasion depends not only on the message itself but on what happens in the moments immediately before it is delivered, a concept he calls pre-suasion. By directing an audience's attention strategically beforehand, a communicator can prime receptivity to a request without altering the substance of the argument.

Jason Schreier investigates the systemic instability of the video game industry through firsthand interviews with developers whose studios—responsible for titles including Bioshock Infinite, Dead Space, and Epic Mickey—were shut down despite commercial or critical success. The book examines hostile takeovers, corporate dysfunction, and bounced checks, asking why workers struggle to build stable careers in an industry generating record profits.

Julian Wiseman provides a non-mathematical introduction to fixed-income markets, explaining how deposits, bonds, futures, and swaps are structured and priced, along with the roles played by different participants in those markets. The book targets newcomers to financial services—traders, analysts, lawyers, and accountants—and uses practical examples to explain pricing conventions and trading mechanics.

John Plant, creator of the Primitive Technology YouTube channel, provides step-by-step illustrated instructions for constructing over fifty tools, shelters, and functional objects using only natural materials found in the wild. Each project includes guidance on material sourcing and historical context for the techniques involved.

Judea Pearl develops a rigorous probabilistic framework for reasoning under uncertainty in artificial intelligence, presenting belief networks as a mechanism for combining probability theory with automated inference. The book covers network propagation techniques, connections to alternative uncertainty formalisms such as Dempster-Shafer theory, and applications in diagnosis, forecasting, speech recognition, and decision support.

sources.title: Probabilistic Reasoning in Intelligent Systems | Amazon

synopsis: Geoffrey Grimmett and David Stirzaker present a comprehensive treatment of probability theory and stochastic processes, moving from foundational concepts—random variables, generating functions, and Markov chains—through advanced topics including martingales, diffusion processes, and stochastic calculus. The text spans undergraduate through graduate level, with emphasis on modeling and nearly four hundred exercises.

sources.title: Probability and Random Processes | Oxford University Press

synopsis: Susan Lammers compiles in-depth interviews with nineteen software pioneers—including Charles Simonyi, Dan Bricklin, Bob Frankston, and Andy Hertzfeld—who built the foundational programs of the personal computer era. The interviews probe each programmer's design philosophy, creative process, and approach to solving problems under severe hardware constraints.

Jon Bentley collects essays on practical programming techniques and fundamental design principles, each grown from real problems encountered by working programmers, covering algorithms, data structures, correctness, performance tuning, and code optimization. The essays combine lucid explanations with concrete case studies and exercises intended to sharpen how programmers think about problem decomposition and efficiency.

Set in 2012 at a fictional Internet portal called Boing that is losing ground to Google and Facebook, the novel follows marketing analyst Glenn Cregg as he pitches a new product to reverse the company's decline, only to undermine himself by violating his own principle of objectivity. The satire draws on the author's years working at Yahoo.

Wayne Weiten applies psychological research to practical challenges of everyday life, covering topics such as self-esteem, stress management, relationships, career development, and psychological disorders with an emphasis on helping readers understand and adjust to contemporary social environments. The textbook integrates classic and current research with accessible examples drawn from real-world situations.

sources.title: Psychology Applied to Modern Life | Barnes & Noble

synopsis: Quantum physicist Tyson Klein discovers an encoded signal in data from the Large Hadron Collider at CERN that appears to originate from alternate realities, drawing him and three others into a conspiracy that transports them across parallel worlds where World War II ended differently. The novel blends hard science concepts with alternate-history military thriller elements as the group races to decode the quantum radio's purpose.
